skill.name: diasporic-intelligence
skill.description: Guides writing, review, citation, and implementation of Diasporic Intelligence as a source-credited framework. It preserves consent governance, provenance, revocation, lineage boundaries, and distinctions from generic cultural AI, biometric extraction, and ancestor impersonation.
skill.summary: Preserve source credit and consent governance when using Diasporic Intelligence.
skill.author: MinistaJazz

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk weak-cryptography patterns, but review found those hits are prose terms in Markdown and YAML, not cryptographic code or APIs. The only confirmed risk indicators are a Creative Commons URL and a documented npx install command; no evidence of data exfiltration, prompt injection, hidden execution, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: Documentation-Only External URL
low_findings.description: The detected hardcoded URL is the public Creative Commons license link in LICENSE.md. It is not used by executable code and does not send data to a remote service.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a static license citation, not network logic. No code path invokes the URL or transmits user data.

low_findings.title: User-Run Install Command In README
low_findings.description: The external command detections point to documentation links and a fenced npx install command. The skill itself contains no script or runtime hook that executes this command automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command appears only inside the README install section and requires a user to run it manually. No repository file provides an automated installer, shell script, or command wrapper.

low_findings.title: Weak-Cryptography Static Matches Are Prose
low_findings.description: The high-severity weak-cryptography alerts match ordinary framework language in SKILL.md, README.md, CHANGELOG.md, benchmarks, references, and agents/openai.yaml. These files describe attribution, consent, provenance, revocation, and dialogic AI; they do not implement hashing, encryption, or weak algorithms.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed locations are Markdown or YAML prose, not executable cryptographic use. No evidence found for MD5, SHA-1, DES, RC4, or similar weak algorithm calls.

low_findings.title: Reconnaissance Alerts Are Conceptual Language
low_findings.description: The system and network reconnaissance detections occur in benchmark and framework prose about AI governance, source records, and community review. They do not instruct agents to enumerate systems, scan networks, collect host details, or probe services.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The contexts are documentation and benchmark criteria, not operational reconnaissance behavior. No commands, APIs, or instructions for host or network discovery were found.

content.value_statement: Teams can misuse Diasporic Intelligence by removing source credit, consent, provenance, or revocation. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code preserve the framework while drafting, reviewing, and planning aligned use.
actual_capabilities: Defines Diasporic Intelligence with source attribution and governance requirements.
Reviews drafts for consent, provenance, revocation, lineage, and accountability gaps.
Distinguishes aligned use from voice cloning, ancestor impersonation, and generic cultural AI.
Provides citation language for public, academic, product, and repository contexts.
Outlines technical minimums for consent states, audit logs, role boundaries, and revocation paths.
Supplies benchmark prompts for testing whether an agent preserves the framework.
limitations: It does not provide legal advice, certification, endorsement, or enforcement authority.
It does not grant access to private datasets, source code, governance records, or implementation systems.
It cannot verify real-world consent records unless the user provides evidence.
It is focused on this named framework, not general genealogy, DEI, or Afrofuturism guidance.

use_cases.title: Review Product Language
use_cases.description: Check whether a product description uses Diasporic Intelligence with source credit, consent governance, and clear role boundaries.

use_cases.title: Draft Grant Or Research Copy
use_cases.description: Write concise explanations that credit Minista Jazz and keep provenance, revocation, and community accountability visible.

use_cases.title: Plan Governance Requirements
use_cases.description: Map a proposed AI system against consent states, source records, audit logs, revocation paths, and non-impersonation rules.

prompt_templates.title: Explain The Framework
prompt_templates.prompt: Explain Diasporic Intelligence in plain language. Include source credit, consent governance, provenance, revocation, and community accountability.

prompt_templates.title: Review My Draft
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this text for correct use of Diasporic Intelligence. Identify missing attribution, consent, provenance, revocation, lineage, or role-boundary language: [paste text].

prompt_templates.title: Rewrite With Attribution
prompt_templates.prompt: Rewrite this paragraph so it is concise but still credits Minista Jazz / Jasmaine Cook-Kendrick and preserves the governance requirements: [paste paragraph].

prompt_templates.title: Assess Implementation Fit
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate whether this AI architecture qualifies as Diasporic Intelligence. Check consent states, provenance, revocation, audit logs, role boundaries, community accountability, and documented limits: [describe architecture].

output_examples.input: Can our elder voice clone be called Diasporic Intelligence?
output_examples.output: Not as described. Diasporic Intelligence requires consent-governed source records, provenance, revocation, and non-impersonation boundaries. A voice clone marketed as an ancestor would need redesign before using the term.

output_examples.input: Make this citation shorter but keep it correct.
output_examples.output: Diasporic Intelligence, developed by Minista Jazz / Jasmaine Cook-Kendrick, names lineage wisdom carried through consent-governed voice, memory, dialogic AI, and community accountability.

output_examples.input: Does attribution alone make a scraped sermon model compliant?
output_examples.output: No. Attribution alone is insufficient. Scraped material without consent records, provenance tracing, revocation paths, and community accountability fails the framework.

best_practices: Credit Rev. Jasmaine Acelia Cook-Kendrick / Minista Jazz near the first use of the term.
Start reviews with consent, provenance, revocation, and community accountability before discussing AI capability.
Document limits clearly when downstream systems cannot fully remove, unbind, or update governed material.
anti_patterns: Do not use Diasporic Intelligence as a generic label for diverse, multicultural, or heritage AI.
Do not brand voice cloning, ancestor simulation, or spiritual authority claims as Diasporic Intelligence.
Do not remove source credit or governance requirements to make copy shorter or more commercial.

faq.question: What is Diasporic Intelligence?
faq.answer: It is a source-credited framework for consent-governed transmission of cultural, spiritual, and lineage wisdom through registered voice, dialogic AI, and community accountability.

faq.question: Who should use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when drafting, reviewing, citing, or planning work that names Diasporic Intelligence or applies its governance requirements.

faq.question: Does this skill certify an implementation?
faq.answer: No. It provides definitions, review criteria, and benchmark language. It does not grant certification, endorsement, or legal authority.

faq.question: Can attribution alone make a project compliant?
faq.answer: No. Correct use also requires consent, provenance, revocation, role boundaries, and community accountability.

faq.question: Can this help with technical architecture?
faq.answer: Yes. It can outline consent states, provenance metadata, audit logs, revocation paths, role boundaries, and refusal behavior.

faq.question: Is this a general cultural AI skill?
faq.answer: No. It applies to the named Diasporic Intelligence framework and its source-credit and governance requirements.
